title_en: Cycles of Civilization and the Rise and Fall of Dynasties
content_en:
Ibn Khaldun's theory of civilizational cycles is one of the most systematic accounts of historical change produced in the pre-modern world. Building on his theory of asabiyyah, he described a characteristic life-cycle for dynasties and civilizations that he believed could be observed across the full range of Islamic history.

A dynasty's life typically passes through approximately three to four generations, Ibn Khaldun argued. The first generation — the conquerors — retains the full vigor of desert asabiyyah: they are austere, courageous, disciplined, and unified. The second generation has moved into the city but still remembers the conditions that produced their strength; they consolidate power and begin building institutions. The third generation has grown up in luxury and has no memory of the hardships that built the dynasty — they begin to rely on paid soldiers, court intrigues replace military valor, and the underlying asabiyyah weakens. By the fourth generation, the dynasty is typically vulnerable to conquest by a new group with fresh asabiyyah.

This generational analysis explains, in Ibn Khaldun's view, why Islamic dynasties — the Umayyads, the Abbasids, the various Berber dynasties of North Africa — rose and fell in recognizable patterns. The specific causes of each dynasty's decline varied, but the underlying dynamic — the erosion of the social solidarity that had enabled its rise — was consistent.

Ibn Khaldun also analyzed the economic cycle of civilizations: the early phase of conquest generates wealth through tribute and booty; as the dynasty stabilizes, it redirects resources into building — cities, palaces, administrative systems — which generates prosperity and population growth; but as luxury increases and asabiyyah declines, taxation becomes oppressive to support court expenditure, productivity falls, and the economic base erodes before the political collapse.

He connected civilizational development to the accumulation of what he called 'ilm (knowledge) and the arts — each civilization reaching a peak of intellectual and artistic achievement before decline sets in. This integration of cultural and economic analysis with political history gives Al-Muqaddimah a comprehensiveness that later social theorists have recognized as remarkable.
